hypotheses: The RTL implementation of TopModule does not fully match the reference module's logic for all input combinations, leading to 33 mismatches out of 100 samples [evidence: log_excerpt L9 'Output 'f' has 33 mismatches. First mismatch occurred at time 10.', L13 'Mismatches: 33 in 100 samples'].

Some input cases (x[3:2]=11, x[1:0]=10/11; x[3:2]=10, x[1:0]=11; x[3:2]=00, x[1:0]=00/10) are treated as 'don't care' and assigned 0 in the RTL, but the reference module may implement different behavior for these cases [evidence: RTL L27-31 comments].

The testbench generates random 4-bit inputs, so all possible input combinations are likely exercised; thus, any missing or incorrect case handling in the RTL will be exposed [evidence: TB L13 'x <= $random;'].
likely_failure_points: RTL lines 27-31: Cases marked as 'don't care' and assigned 0 may not match the reference module's output for those input combinations [evidence: RTL L27-31].
RTL may be missing explicit handling for some input patterns that the reference module treats differently, leading to output mismatches [evidence: log_excerpt L9, L13; RTL L27-32].
recommended_probes: Add probes to monitor the input x and both f_dut and f_ref at every cycle to identify which input patterns cause mismatches.
Instrument the testbench to print x, f_dut, and f_ref whenever a mismatch is detected (i.e., when tb_mismatch is high).
Add a coverage monitor to count how many times each x[3:2] and x[1:0] combination occurs and whether mismatches cluster around specific patterns.
analysis_notes: The evidence strongly suggests that the RTL's handling of 'don't care' cases (assigned 0) does not match the reference module's behavior for those input patterns, resulting in mismatches. The testbench is robust and randomizes all input combinations, so any missing or incorrect case in the RTL will be detected. However, without the reference module's source, we cannot confirm exactly how it handles the 'don't care' cases. Probing the mismatched input patterns will quickly clarify which cases are problematic. No prior attempts or patch strategies exist, so this is a first-pass analysis.
